Don’t know where to start? We consulted with trusted dermatologists and hair experts, experimented with options on our own fine strands, and reviewed hundreds of customer reviews to compile the 10 best shampoos for fine hair. If your fine hair could use a volume boost, the formulas below will get the job done.

The Ouai brand is known for adding protein to your strands, but what you didn’t know is that it caters to all hair types and textures. If you’re looking to add body and life to your hair, the Fine Hair Shampoo takes care of your hair straight from the roots. “It has a lightweight formula with ingredients like keratin, biotin, and chia seed that gently cleanses while keeping hair soft, smooth, and full of movement,” saysLiz Owusu, founder of the renownedLiz Loves Hairsalon in Brooklyn. The formula clarifies and volumizes to give you a salon-worthy finish in the comfort of your home.
Size: 10 fl oz
Ouairating:4.6/5 stars
An ELLE editor says:“My hair, in its natural state, becomes lank and can trend towardsShrek’s Lord Farquaad very quickly. It also tends to get greasy easily at the roots, so a lot of overly moisturizing shampoos can weigh it down. This is a nice, everyday shampoo for fine hair that gives my hair a lot of nice body and lift, even when I just air-dry it. Like all Ouai products, it has a Byredo-level of scent, so I feel like I’m walking in a cloud of good hair perfume all day.”—Kathleen Hou, ELLE beauty director

This Pureology pick features wheat protein, which promotes volume, and eucalyptus leaf extract, which nourishes hair follicles and helps your strands grow. Plus, despite its cleansing prowess, the sulfate-free formula protects color-treated hair, allowing your color to stay vibrant for as long as possible.
Size: 8.45 fl oz
Ulta rating: 3.6/5 stars
An ELLE editor says: “My fine hair is also color-treated, and I like my hair colorist, so I can’t use just any old shampoo. To maintain a good relationship with my colorist and my hair, I like this shampoo from Pureology. The whole line is formulated to be color-safe, so I don’t need to worry that it will wash my colorist’s hard work down the drain. It also makes my hair feel extra soft, yet not limp.”—Kathleen Hou

R+Co Dallas Biotin Thickening Shampoo is the holy grail of thickening shampoos. Why? All credit goes to the MVPs of the formula: coconut oil, biotin, vitamin B5, and saw palmetto berry extract. Each key ingredient works to strengthen, nourish, and encourage a healthy scalp, which in turn promotes growth.
Size: 8.5 fl oz
R+Co Dallas rating:4.4/5 stars
A R+Co Dallas reviewer says:“A staple for my thin hair! I find a lot of thickening shampoo and/or conditioners will weigh my hair down, but this isn’t the case with R+Co. Have been buying and will buy again!”

Because fine hair tends to be more fragile, it’s also extra vulnerable to heat damage. Oribe’s Gold Lust Repair & Restore Shampoo strengthens and nourishes strands with a winning combination of biotin, caffeine, and collagen.
Size: 8.5 fl oz
Sephora rating:4.3/5 stars
A beauty expert says:“My hair feels incredibly soft and silky whenever I try this shampoo. If I didn’t have so many wonderful formulas at my disposal, I might be tempted to use them most of the time. Plus, the fragrance—with notes of bergamot, jasmine, and sandalwood—is sophisticated and gorgeous.”—Carol Lee, beauty writer

Here’s a shampoo that’s free of all the bad (sulfates, parabens) and packed with the good (Caprauna turnip extract, which is rich in vitamins). This best-selling shampoo turns up the volume by gently cleansing away build-up to make hair feel lightweight and bouncy.
Size: 8.45 fl oz
Amazon rating: 4.2/5 stars
An Amazon reviewer says: “I've used Davines’s Love shampoo for years, but my hair texture changed, and it was no longer working for me. My stylist suggested a shampoo for fine hair, so I looked to see if Davines made one. Volu has the same characteristics I enjoyed from Love—simple packaging, use only a tiny amount, luxurious foam, and rinses out very easily! And now, my fine hair looks so much fuller and better.”

The Living Proof Full Shampoo gently cleanses your hair by removing buildup and preventing an oily scalp to encourage fuller hair. “This silicone-free shampoo creates airy fullness and long-lasting volume with a soft, touchable feel,” says Owusu. It gives your hair a natural boost without weighing it down. Not only will this shampoo make your hair feel so full and voluminous, but it will leave your locks soft to the touch.
Size: 8 fl oz
Amazonrating:4.5/5 stars
An Amazon reviewer says:“This is the best shampoo I have ever used. Other shampoos can make my hair greasy again even on the same day. My hair is also very thin and greasy. With this shampoo, I made it to five days before it even looked greasy at all!”

This clean shampoo eschews traditional surfactants, which can strip strands of beneficial oils, in favor of a gentle coconut-derived surfactant system. The formula also incorporates Tsubaki seed oil to hydrate the strands, but it’s not so heavy that it’ll weigh down fine strands. After your shower, your scalp and hair will feel squeaky clean, and your tresses will smell like fresh yuzu, bergamot, and lemongrass.
Size: 8.1 fl oz
Sephorarating:4/5 stars
A beauty expert says:“As someone with fine and oily hair, I’m so afraid of weighing down and over-hydrating my strands. I’m so nervous about it, in fact, that I’ll forego conditioner most days (don’t tell my stylists!). This shampoo is light enough that it doesn’t overload my hair with moisture, which allows me to go in with a pump of leave-in conditioner after my showers to keep my locks smooth and shiny. I also feel like it lifts my roots for a more volumized appearance.”—Carol Lee

“I love that fine-hair shampoos can transform limp strands into full, airy volume without weighing them down,” says celebrity hairstylist Nick Stenson. “The Volume Shampoo is perfect for fine hair because it adds instant volume and luster to fine-to-normal, color-treated hair, while sealing in moisture, providing heat protection, and leaving hair looking fuller and more vibrant.”
Size:12 fl oz.
Ulta rating:4.2/5
An Ulta reviewer says:“Great shampoo. I have thin, wavy hair with no weight to the strands, and this shampoo leaves my hair with a nice texture—not over soft nor tangled. However, the bottle is extremely poorly designed. There is a lot of wastage!”

“This one’s a current standout,” says Owusu. The Amika Big Hit Volumizing Shampoo is powered by glycerin, sea buckthorn, and caffeine to rejuvenate the strands while boosting body. “The texture and scent are incredible, and it gives fine hair that healthy, bouncy look everyone’s chasing right now,” she adds.
Size:9.2 fl oz.
Amazon rating:4.7/5
An Amazon reviewer says:“I have fine hair and decided to give this shampoo a shot. I've not switched shampoo in three months because of how soft it makes my hair. The quality is fantastic and it smells floral to me, which I love. Brushing my hair out after using both this and the matching conditioner is like a dream with barely any tangles. My hair shines like I just left the salon when it's dry.”

You need a shampoo in your life that will plump your hair to the max. The Bondi Boost Thickening Therapy Shampoo is made with biotin to support healthy hair growth. If you have fine hair and are concerned about it breaking, this shampoo contains amaranth extract, which helps strengthen your hair.
Size: 16.9 fl oz
Amazon rating:4.4/5 stars
An Amazon reviewer says:“A rich shampoo...you only need a dime's worth and your hair will suds up and feel thick!”

Dr. Mona Goharais a board-certified dermatologist based in Hamden, CT.
Dr. Ellen Marmur, board-certified dermatologist and founder ofMMSkincare.
Liz Owusuis the founder of theLiz Loves Hairsalon in Brooklyn, NY.
Nick Stensonis a celebrity hair stylist and the founder of Nick Stenson Beauty.

trend_name: What shampoo ingredients are especially helpful for those with fine hair?
description: Dr. Ellen Marmur, board-certified dermatologist and founder ofMMSkincare, emphasizes that hydration is paramount when it comes to fine hair. “Choose formulas that feature hyaluronic acid, vitamin E, and proteins to lock in moisture and help strengthen the strands. I would also avoid any formulas containing sulfates as these strip the natural oil produced in our hair; and for the finer hair types, they need all the moisture retention they can get,” she says. Dr. Marmur also recommends looking for products that contain polymers, which can “aid thickness and better lift the hair shaft,” she adds.

trend_name: Other than shampoo, what other products should those with fine hair use to keep their hair healthy?
description: If you have fine hair that gets oily easily, you might be reluctant to incorporate a hair oil into your routine—but Dr. Marmur insists that they’ll become your greatest ally. “Start by incorporating these into your routine, and with consistent use you’ll see improvement. For fine hair, I recommendMielle Rosemary Mint Scalp & Hair Strengthening Oil—this features rosemary, castor oil, and biotin, a nutrient-rich blend to help freshen the hair, smooth split ends, and help with dry scalp (common among people with fine hair).”

As a leading publisher of fashion, lifestyle, and beauty content, ELLE.com is committed to highlighting the best products in various categories by personally testing the latest and most innovative products, interviewing countless experts, and vetting customer-loved items. For this piece, beauty writersCarol Leeand Emma Aerin Beckerspoke with trusted hair experts about shampoo. They also searched the internet for top-rated shampoos for fine hair, perused reviews for dozens of popular options, and evaluated each on their features and customer reviews.

For this piece, beauty writersCarol Leeand Emma Aerin Beckerspoke with trusted hair experts and dermatologists to learn all about the best shampoos for fine hair. They also tested them out, read through countless customer reviews, and evaluated each product on their unique features.

TheAirwrap i.d., theAirstrait, theCorrale, theSupersonic Nural, theSupersonic r, theAirwrap Origin, and theSupersonic Originare discounted for Black Friday, and ahead we’ll break down where you can get the best deal and exclusive colorways.

trend_name: Dyson Airwrap i.d. Multi-styler and Dryer
description:
Dyson’s most advanced Airwrap model iscurrently 23 percent off on Amazon, Sephora, and Nordstrom.It’s also 23 percent off atUlta, but only if you get thespecial edition model, which features a Red Velvet colorway. Sephora carries a special edition shade too—it’s calledJasper Plum.
The bluetooth-enabled hair tool lets you have complete setting customization tailored to your hair type and texture to minimize heat damage.
The straight and wavy hair setcomes with the conical barrel, 1.6-inch long barrel, large round volumizing brush, a smoothing paddle brush, a flyaway smoothing dryer, and a fast dryer attachment.The curly and coily setcomes with a diffuser, a firm smoothing brush, and a wide-tooth comb attachment, along with the conical barrel, large round volumizing brush, and the fast dryer attachment.

Dyson’s scalp-protecting hair dryer is27 percent off(which is nearly $150 off)at bothSephoraandAmazonin Amber Silk. You can also get deals on Sephora’slimited-edition Jasper Plumand theTeal/Orange colorway, as well as theUlta-exclusive Red Velvet shade.
The advanced hair dryer has a network of sensors that can detect when you’re close to your scalp and reduce heat levels accordingly. It can also identify which attachment you’re using and adjust the settings for optimal performance. The hair dryer comes with five attachments: a styling concentrator, a flyaway smoother, a wide tooth comb, a wave and curl diffuser, and a gentle air/rough drying attachment.

trend_name: Dyson Supersonic r Professional hair dryer
description: Dyson’s lightest hair dryer iscurrently$200 off,exclusively on its site. Specifically engineered for stylists, this hair dryer features a curved, ergonomic design that helps take a load off your arms and wrists. It delivers precision for easy control, even, powerful heating and airflow, and a longer cable. It has four heat settings and a cold shot, along with five attachments.

You can snag Dyson’s Airstrait alsoon sale at 27 percent offatAmazon,Nordstrom,Ulta, andSephorain the Amber Silk colorway—however, Amazon has the originalnickle/copper hueand Ulta has theRed Velvet shade on sale, too.
The Airstrait offers wet-to-dry straightening by using hot air in a  concentrated area rather than hot plates to minimize heat damage. “Regardless of hair type, the finished look with the Airstrait is a softer take on straight hair. Think less the pin-straight hair seen onDua Lipa on the spring 2022 Versace runwayand more the bodifiedGigi Hadid style seen at the Versace Los Angeles show,” saysformer Elle beauty editor Margaux Anbouba in her review.

Save$140 on the Corrale straighteneratAmazon,Sephora, andUlta. Create curls, waves, and sleek styles using its flexible plates that helps minimize damage and breakage. It has three precise heat settings: 330°F, 365°F and 410°F. The travel-friendly hair tool offers 30 minutes of wireless styling, too.

Dyson’s original Airwrap model is 27 percent off only atSephora—you save around $150. It comes with the Coanda smoothing dryer, perfect for rough drying and taming flyaways, 1.2-inch Airwrap barrel for luscious waves and curls, and a round volumizing brush attachment for salon-quality blowouts.

If you prefer to upgrade your hair dryer without the bells and whistles of multiple attachments, the Supersonic Hair Dryer is 33 percent off onDyson’s site.It comes with three attachments: a diffuser, a wide tooth comb, and a smoothing nozzle.
However, models featuring five attachments, with the addition of a flyaway attachment and a gentle air attachment, are also on sale for 27 percent off atAmazon,Nordstrom, andUlta.
With a powerful motor, it can dry hair quickly while minimizing heat damage with fast. It has three heat settings, three speed settings, and a cold shot button for easy, simplified styling.

trend_name: Taylor Swift Embraces the '90s With Her 'Opalite' Music Video Cocoa Brown Bob
description:
Taylor Swifthas unveiled yet another dramatic hair transformation for herLife of a Showgirlera, debuting a faded chocolate-brown lob paired with curtain bangs for her new music video, “Opalite. ” The pop star revealed her nostalgic new look via Instagram in a carousel of ’90s-inspired stills from the video, with the cropped length and softened color playing a vital role in creating an authentic vintage feel.
The shade itself sits between a natural brunette and a dusty faux-chocolate shade, while her cut allows soft natural texture to come through. Ashoulder-brushing lengthandsoft angled fringegive a soft, flattering shape to the look. Not to mention, it’s a deliberate shift from Taylor’s go-to blonde look.
The video sees Swift joined by actorsCillian Murphy, Domhnall Gleeson,Greta Lee, andJodie Turner-Smith, alongside singer Lewis Capaldi, all of whom also appeared with the star on a recent episode of The Graham Norton Show.
“When we were all talking during the broadcast, Domhnall made a lighthearted joke about wanting to be in one of my music videos,” Swift recalls in her Instagram caption. “He’s Irish! He was joking! Except that in that moment during the interview, I was instantly struck with an idea. And so, a week later, he received an email with a script I’d written for the Opalite video, where he was playing the starring role.”
“I had this thought that it would be wild if all of our fellow guests on The Graham Norton Show that night — including Graham himself — could be a part of it too,” she continues. “Like a school group project, but for adults, and it isn’t mandatory. To my delight, everyone from the show made the effort to time-travel back to the ’90s with us and help bring this video to life.”
Swift’s latest color isn’t the only beauty detail drawn directly from the ’90s. The stark departure from her trademark blonde shade is accompanied by an array of iconic throwback accessories. Think butterfly clips,claw clips, and scrunchies galore. If you’ve been considering bringing a playful edge to your look in 2026, these joyful hair favorites now come officially Swift-approved.

trend_name: Parfums de Marly Valaya Hair Mist
description:
The luxe white and silver bottle offers fresh notes of bergamot, mandarin, and sweet white peach. And while theValaya Eau de Parfumbottle costs a whopping $250, the hair-forward formula packs the same scent for just $97—a helpful fragrance hack that can stay between us.
Kardashian’s scented hair certainly didn’t stop there. As she embarked on a quick shopping spree in head-to-toe vintage Roberto Cavalli, she also flaunted an “Aspen-chic” blowout created by Giannetos.
“I wanted to create a bouncy and soft look for Kim. It’s Aspen blowout chic!” he says. “For the final touch, I usedParfums de Marly Delina Hair Perfumeto make the hair smell delicious.”

trend_name: Parfums de Marly  Delina Hair Mist
description:
Just like with the previous scent, you can score the same feminine floral notes for over $150 less than theEau de Parfumprice.
Shop both hair perfumes for locks that smell just as glamorous as they look. Plus, who wouldn’t want to smell like a Kardashian anyway?

It also ties back toSinnersitself, a supernatural Southern Gothic set in Jim Crow-era Mississippi that explores Black culture, music, and folklore passed down through history. The film went on to earn a record-breaking 16 Oscar nominations—the most ever for a single film—and won four Academy Awards Sunday night: Best Actor forMichael B. Jordan, Best Original Screenplay for Ryan Coogler, Best Original Score for Ludwig Göransson, and Best Cinematography for Autumn Durald Arkapaw.
The cast’s statement-making hairstyles also highlight the meticulous craft of the women behind them, in a space long defined by Eurocentric beauty standards.
When Mosaku prepped for the London stretch of theSinnerspress tour, she turned to friend and longtime collaboratorDionne Smith, an international celebrity hairstylist. (Araxi Lindseyoversaw her looks during press in North America.) “She was like, ‘Dionne, I want something that talks to the ancestors. I want African-inspired. Everything that we’ve done had some type of braid element,’” Smith recalls. “It was just such a beautiful thing to see.”
For the BAFTAs, Smith even threaded small rings into Mosaku’s braids after noticing the details in her gown. From the front, the style featured four afro puffs that resembled a butterfly. “Everything for me is intentional,” she says. “I saw the swirls in the dress and thought the rings would complement that shape and catch the light on the red carpet—and on stage if she won.” Sure enough, Mosaku took home the award for Best Supporting Actress that night.
The woman behind Ryan Coogler’s press-tour braids is his usual braiderTyzanna Bailey, who also happens to be his cousin. Bailey started doing hair in childhood and became the go-to person in the family for braided hairstyles.
Coogler’s looks, on average, took around three hours to create. “We always do hearts, but the hearts during the press run correlated to the ‘fellowship and love’ line said by Remmick in the movie,” Bailey says. Other design flourishes have included a braided cross, and a guitar and treble clef for the Oscars—a nod to how music is threaded throughout the film while also reflecting Coogler’s love for music.
“I’m glad to see braids becoming more of a thing that we’re getting comfortable wearing for those big moments,” Bailey says. “Braids definitely communicate something that other hairstyles can’t. I wish more people felt comfortable wearing braids and expressing themselves through them.
“I feel braids represent history, identity, creativity, and tradition—a tradition that has been passed down through generations. It’s a form of cultural expression. For some it’s storytelling; wearing braids can connect to heritage while also being able to show off your own style and creativity at the same time.”
Hollywood has long rehearsed a narrow script for what a leading lady should be: thin, desirable, unthreatening, and fair-skinned, or at least as close to whiteness as casting will allow. Her hair falls straight or is obediently styled. The subtext has remained consistent: Not too loud. Not too political. Not too much. And certainly not too Black.
This version of femininity—and the expectation that women comply with it—can be deeply emotionally draining and traumatic. It requires a constant adjusting of the body: smoothing the hair, softening the voice, sanding down the edges. For many women, it’s a daily negotiation between what feels natural and what feels acceptable.
And when someone refuses that script, the reaction can be telling.
In 2015, when then 18-year-old Zendaya appeared on the Oscars red carpet wearing faux locs—a traditional Black hairstyle—television host Giuliana Rancic joked that they probably smelled like “patchouli oil or weed.” The comment drewswift backlashand spotlighted the casual racism Black hair has long been subjected to in public spaces.
For years on Hollywood sets, department heads have failed to deliver proper care and treatment for Black hair, leaving actors to shoulder theemotional and physical laborof preparing their own hair or navigating an environment thatdoesn’t fully supporttheir styling needs. Though this isslowly shifting, the change is long overdue.
The scrutiny isn’t limited to Hollywood. In schools, workplaces, and public institutions across the United States, natural hairstyles like braids, locs, and afros have long been treated as distractions or violations of dress codes. The passage of theCROWN Actin 2019 in multiple states—legislation designed to prohibit hair discrimination—exists precisely because those biases have had real-life consequences.
Long before the stars hit the red carpet, the work begins in hotel suites, living rooms, and backstage dressing areas. Their stylists get up close and personal, fingers moving methodically as combs carve geometric sections into their scalps, pulling strands taut, each loop and twist carrying hours of patience and ancestral knowledge. The process generally takes hours—sometimes days, depending on the complexity of the style. Patterns must be mapped and sectioned. The scalp becomes a canvas.
“Braids are a striking look, kind of like when someone colors their hair,” says Los Angeles-based hairstylistEvalyn Denis, who created Caton’s stitch braid style.
When Caton began thinking about how he wanted to present himself during theSinnerspress run, Denis says the process quickly became collaborative.
“He did a great job of giving me a baseline of what he was going for,” she explains. “He has a haircut within his ’fro, and we ended up doing these really wavy zigzag tiny braids with stitch braids alongside the six braids we landed on.”
The design typically takes two to three hours. But the full process on Caton—washing, blow-drying, and braiding—took about five and a half, says Denis. “It was cool to see him not just be like, ‘Can I just do some classic straightbacks?’ When he pops out, it’s clean and it’s beautiful.”
A braided hairstyle can also be a meaningful celebration of self, explains Denis. “It really does show off your features. You’re not hiding behind hair. That’s extremely important and it sets the tone for confidence, even if you’re nervous.”
Still, the technical skill behind braiding is often misunderstood within the beauty industry. In cosmetology programs, Denis says, braiding is often treated as a minor subsection of hairstyling rather than a discipline in its own right.
“There’s still a technical side to braiding that I don’t know is truly recognized legally,” she says. “It’s not just a chapter in a book—it could be a whole semester.”
That gap in recognition can appear in subtler ways too: unrealistic booking timelines, lacking credit for glam teams on editorial shoots, or the lingering assumption that braiding is somehow less complex than other forms of hairstyling.
“It’s tough to just say [you’re a] ‘braider,’” says Denis, who studied cut, color, and the gamut of haircare in cosmetology school. “There’s a connotation there unfortunately. It locks you into one category when the work actually involves so many different skills.”
“I’ve intentionally said I’m not a braider,” adds Smith, who was also formally trained in cosmetology school. “A lot of braiders get classed as unprofessional hairdressers because many of them only braid and some are self-taught—and there’s nothing wrong with that. I used to braid my sisters’ hair—that’s how I learned. I wasn’t trained in braiding. I just had two sisters and traditional Caribbean parents who said, ‘You look after your sisters and do their hair for the school week. That’s your duty as the big sister.’”
At the same time, she notes, braided styles require far more technical knowledge than many people realize.
“You’re working with hair, so you have to understand the science of it,” Smith says. “If someone got a relaxer a week ago, they shouldn’t have braids until another two weeks. If the hair is pulled too tight, if the direction of the braids is wrong—those things can cause damage.” There’s a real skill to doing it properly, but braiders could also benefit from more technical support from across the industry, Smith adds.
“Braids take time; they take patience. It’s almost like trying to create a masterpiece strand by strand,” Bailey says. But the hard work that goes into braided hairstyles, especially those on the red carpet this season, pays off in an impactful way. “I think Ryan has done a great job showing how important it is to represent culture and still have the best style while doing it. When you think of Ryan, the first thing you probably think of are his braids and his mustache.”
A braided crown at a premiere or awards show might seem like a simple styling choice, but to someone watching from home, it affirms self-acceptance, reminding us that the definition of beauty has never been singular—and it doesn’t have to be.
You don’t have to be an Oscar nominee to embrace the style either. When it comes to getting braids—whether it’s for everyday wear or special occasions—the consensus is to go for it.
“It’s one of those looks that’s timeless,” says Denis. “So you can’t ever go wrong with having a braided look on any carpet, and I’m glad that now we’re at a place where people love it.”

trend_name: Who Is the Feathered Bob Best For?
description: Hailey Bieber is the latest adopter of this hairstyle, showcasing her shoulder-length brunette hair with gently feathered ends. “The feathered bob has movement and due to its cutting techniques, it allows your natural hair texture to shine while creating a beautiful silhouette,” says Hawkins.

When it comes to tailoring the cut, Hawkins says that it is about “the distribution of weight within your haircut.” A thorough consultation, as always, is key, as there are multiple ways of ensuring your resulting style looks balanced and brings out the best in your natural texture.

trend_name: The Feathered Bob vs. the French Bob
description: Both have a lived-in, relaxed feel, so it is easy to confuse the two. But generally, the feathered style is significantly lighter. “It’s more intentional. The weight is more directed at the root, leaving a lot more softness throughout the length and ends,” Hawkins tells ELLE.

trend_name: How to Style the Feathered Bob
description: As one of the more low-key, understated cuts, your daily styling routine should be relatively easy. If air-drying is your preferred method, run a leave-in conditioning treatment to minimize frizz and encourage shine. You can also use a diffuser to restore definition to curls, or use a hot brush for a smoother effect.

According to McKnight, the spring/summer runway is dominated by sleek styles. Whether that presents itself in a smooth, ultra-glossy finish or a more daring “wet” look, “sculpted, architectural silhouettes that feel modern and polished” will be all the rage.
Striking a harmony of shine and structure, this bob is defined by controlled styling with minimal flyaways, making it ideal for those with straight or fine hair looking for an effortlessly bold look.

Those who enjoyed a short bob in 2025 may look to the “cloud bob” as a natural next step in the grow-out stage, says Cusick, who calls it “the bob that makes you look expensive without trying.” Chicer, longer, and more luxurious than a blunt crop, “This cut grazes the collarbone with softer, rounded edges that sit around the face like a cloud,”she explains.
According to James, this bob may be particularly attractive to those who want to lean into their natural texture without overdoing volume or underplaying it.
Ask your stylist for a midi-length that sits between the collarbone and shoulder for an equally chic and weightless bob with gentle movement; for day-to-day styling, a wave spray and a diffuser will keep the look light and airy.

“Beautifully groomed, effortlessly luxe lengths with movement that gleams,” says McKnight, of what we’re calling the “glassy bob.” Defined by its high-shine finish, which celebrates healthy-looking hair and a modern, refined style, this one works for all hair types.
“Even a very simple cut is elevated with this level of glassy shine, which photographs incredibly well (which is why it’s everywhere on socials),” explains James. Before blow-drying, simply spray a shine-enhancing mist throughout damp hair, then go over with wide irons to get that reflective effect.

Cusick predicts a shift for curlier hair types in 2026, with texture taking the lead. This bob is a prime example: shaped by a natural curl pattern, its silhouette sits higher, creating a softly sculpted illusion.
“Curls lift around the cheekbones while coils gain height through the crown, so the whole look feels intentional and expressive,” says Cusick.
“Ask your stylist to cut your hair in its natural state—either dry or lightly diffused—so you can see the real movement. It needs gentle interior shaping and a perimeter that follows your curl pattern rather than fighting it; the shape is already doing the work for you.”

James suggests that a desire for clean, simple, and intentional haircuts has paved the way for the box bob to trend in 2026. Its versatility is its strength: wear it sleek or with relaxed bends, and it will hold its shape. The result comes from layered and tapered, rather than blunt, ends, which “makes you look quickly put together on days when you really haven’t tried.”

“If you want to instantly update your bob for 2026, add bangs,” advises Cusick. A gentle curtain shape that sits shorter in the middle and longer at the temples (with feathered layers that flick back and frame the face) feels French- and Seventies-inspired, but, most importantly, allows for softer movement instead of a heavy, blunt look.
“Ask for graduated bangs that sit around the bridge of your nose,” adds Cusick. “This is brilliant for fine-to-medium hair because the movement gives you volume.”

In the new year, refreshing your bob won’t only rely on cutting, but also on a strategic use of color. “This bob is all about soft, tonal layering, which makes the cut look fuller and shinier,” says Cusick—adding that this coloring technique works on any bob style or hair type.
Dimension and multitonal shades are key: “think subtle depth at the roots, a gentle mix of tones throughout the mid-lengths, and a hint of brightness at the ends.”
